standard.notes: 'Mon quartier' = my neighbourhood. 'Un quartier calme' = a quiet neighbourhood. In Paris, each 'arrondissement' contains several quartiers. 'Le quartier latin' = the Latin Quarter (famous Parisian district).
examples: J'habite dans un quartier calme.
I live in a quiet neighbourhood.
Le quartier est très agréable.
The neighbourhood is very pleasant.

standard.notes: 'Le boulanger / la boulangère' = the baker. A boulangerie must bake bread on-site to use the name legally. France has around 33,000 boulangeries. 'Aller à la boulangerie' = to go to the bakery.
examples: Il y a une boulangerie au coin de la rue.
There's a bakery on the corner of the street.
J'achète du pain à la boulangerie tous les jours.
I buy bread at the bakery every day.

standard.notes: 'Aller au parc' = to go to the park. 'Un parc public' = a public park. 'Se promener dans le parc' = to walk in the park. French cities typically have many well-maintained public parks.
examples: Les enfants jouent dans le parc.
The children play in the park.
Il y a un grand parc près de chez moi.
There's a big park near my home.

standard.notes: 'La Poste' is the national postal service in France. 'Aller à la poste' = to go to the post office. 'Un bureau de poste' = a post office branch. Also a useful landmark for giving directions.
examples: La poste est ouverte le samedi matin.
The post office is open on Saturday morning.
Il y a une poste dans mon quartier.
There's a post office in my neighbourhood.

standard.notes: Feminine: 'la voisine'. 'Les voisins' = the neighbours. 'Chez les voisins' = at the neighbours' place. 'La Fête des voisins' = Neighbours' Day (a French tradition celebrated in May).
examples: Mon voisin est très gentil.
My neighbour is very nice.
Nous avons de bons voisins.
We have good neighbours.

examples: J'habite dans un quartier calme.
I live in a quiet neighbourhood.
Le quartier est très agréable.
The neighbourhood is very pleasant.

advanced.notes: 'Le boulanger / la boulangère' = the baker. A boulangerie must bake bread on-site to use the name legally. France has around 33,000 boulangeries. 'Aller à la boulangerie' = to go to the bakery.
examples: Il y a une boulangerie au coin de la rue.
There's a bakery on the corner of the street.
J'achète du pain à la boulangerie tous les jours.
I buy bread at the bakery every day.

advanced.notes: 'Aller au parc' = to go to the park. 'Un parc public' = a public park. 'Se promener dans le parc' = to walk in the park. French cities typically have many well-maintained public parks.
examples: Les enfants jouent dans le parc.
The children play in the park.
Il y a un grand parc près de chez moi.
There's a big park near my home.

advanced.notes: 'La Poste' is the national postal service in France. 'Aller à la poste' = to go to the post office. 'Un bureau de poste' = a post office branch. Also a useful landmark for giving directions.
examples: La poste est ouverte le samedi matin.
The post office is open on Saturday morning.
Il y a une poste dans mon quartier.
There's a post office in my neighbourhood.

advanced.notes: Feminine: 'la voisine'. 'Les voisins' = the neighbours. 'Chez les voisins' = at the neighbours' place. 'La Fête des voisins' = Neighbours' Day (a French tradition celebrated in May).
examples: Mon voisin est très gentil.
My neighbour is very nice.
Nous avons de bons voisins.
We have good neighbours.

advanced.meaning: the corner / the area / the spot
advanced.notes: 'Au coin de la rue' = on the corner of the street. 'Un coin tranquille' = a quiet spot. 'Du coin' = local: 'le café du coin' = the local café. 'Les gens du coin' = the locals.
examples: La boulangerie est au coin de la rue.
The bakery is on the corner of the street.
C'est un bon coin pour se promener.
It's a good spot for a walk.

advanced.meaning: the town hall / the city hall
advanced.notes: 'Le maire' = the mayor. 'Se marier à la mairie' = to get married at the town hall (civil marriages in France must take place at the mairie). Used for official documents like birth certificates, ID cards, etc.
examples: La mairie est sur la place principale.
The town hall is on the main square.
Il faut aller à la mairie pour le passeport.
You need to go to the town hall for the passport.

dialogue: J'habite dans un quartier calme, près du parc.
Oui ! Il y a une boulangerie — j'en achète du pain tous les matins.
Oui, il y en a une à cinq minutes. Et un supermarché aussi.
Et les voisins, ils sont comment ?
J'en ai trois sur mon étage. Ils sont très gentils !

questions.question: Where does speaker B live?
options: In a quiet neighbourhood near the park
questions.correctAnswer: In a quiet neighbourhood near the park

questions.question: What does speaker B buy at the bakery every morning?
How many neighbours does speaker B have on their floor?

standard.summary: Speaker B lives in a quiet neighbourhood near a park. The area has a bakery where B buys bread every morning, a post office five minutes away, and a supermarket. Speaker B has three neighbours on the same floor, and they are all very nice.

dialogue: Je viens d'emménager dans le quartier. Tu le connais bien ?
Oui, j'y habite depuis cinq ans ! C'est un quartier très agréable.
Bien sûr ! Il y en a deux. La meilleure est au coin de la rue Victor Hugo.
Il y en a un très grand, juste derrière la mairie. Les voisins s'y retrouvent le dimanche.
Il y a une poste dans le coin ?
Oui, il y en a une sur la place, à côté de la mairie. Elle est ouverte le matin.
Et les voisins, ils sont sympathiques ?
Oui, j'en connais plusieurs. On fait la Fête des voisins en mai — tu vas adorer !

questions.question: How long has speaker B lived in the neighbourhood?
How many bakeries are there in the neighbourhood?
What event does speaker B mention?

advanced.summary: Speaker A just moved to the neighbourhood and asks speaker B for advice. B has lived there for five years. The neighbourhood has two bakeries (the best one on the corner of rue Victor Hugo), a large park behind the town hall where neighbours gather on Sundays, and a post office on the square next to the town hall. B knows several neighbours and mentions the annual Neighbours' Day celebration in May.

standard.title: The Pronoun En (Replacing de + Noun / Expressing Quantity)
standard.explanation:
The pronoun **en** replaces a noun introduced by **de** (du, de la, de l', des) or replaces a **quantity expression**.

**When to use en:**

**1. Replacing 'de + noun' (some/any):**
- Tu veux **du pain** ? → Tu **en** veux ? (Do you want some?)
- J'achète **des croissants**. → J'**en** achète. (I'm buying some.)

**2. Replacing a quantity (the number stays!):**
- Il y a **trois boulangeries**. → Il y **en** a **trois**. (There are three.)
- J'ai **deux voisins**. → J'**en** ai **deux**. (I have two.)
- Tu veux **beaucoup de pain** ? → Tu **en** veux **beaucoup** ? (Do you want a lot?)

**Where en goes:** Before the verb (same position as y, le, la, les).

**With negation:**
- Je n'**en** veux **pas**. (I don't want any.)
- Il n'y **en** a **pas**. (There aren't any.)

**Key expressions with en:**
- **Il y en a** = there is/are some (replaces il y a + des/du)
- **J'en ai** = I have some
- **J'en veux** = I want some

examples.french: Tu veux du pain ? — Oui, j'en veux.
examples.english: Do you want some bread? — Yes, I want some.

examples.french: Il y a des parcs ? — Oui, il y en a deux.
examples.english: Are there parks? — Yes, there are two.

examples.french: Tu as des voisins ? — Oui, j'en ai trois.
examples.english: Do you have neighbours? — Yes, I have three.

drills.question: Complete: Tu veux du café ? — Oui, j'___ veux. (Yes, I want some.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces 'du café' (partitive). J'en veux = I want some.

drills.question: How do you say 'There are three' using en?
drills.explanation: 'En' goes before 'a' in 'il y a': il y en a trois.

drills.question: Complete: Tu as des voisins ? — Oui, j'___ ai cinq. (Yes, I have five.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces 'des voisins'. The number stays: j'en ai cinq.

drills.question: What does 'Je n'en veux pas' mean?
drills.explanation: Negation: ne + en + verb + pas. Je n'en veux pas = I don't want any.

drills.question: Complete: Il y a une boulangerie ? — Oui, il y ___ a une. (Yes, there is one.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces the noun in quantity expressions. Il y en a une = There is one.

advanced.title: The Pronoun En — Quantity, de + Noun & Fixed Expressions
advanced.explanation:
**'En' replaces:**

**1. Partitive articles (du, de la, de l', des) + noun:**
- Tu veux **du pain** ? → Tu **en** veux ?
- Elle mange **de la salade**. → Elle **en** mange.
- Il boit **de l'eau**. → Il **en** boit.

**2. Quantity expressions (number/adverb stays, noun goes):**
- J'ai **trois chats**. → J'**en** ai **trois**.
- Il y a **beaucoup de parcs**. → Il y **en** a **beaucoup**.
- Tu veux **un peu de sucre** ? → Tu **en** veux **un peu** ?
- Je n'ai **pas de voisins**. → Je n'**en** ai **pas**.

**3. De + noun (after verbs that use 'de'):**
- Tu parles **de ton quartier** ? → Tu **en** parles ? (Are you talking about it?)
- J'ai besoin **d'une carte**. → J'**en** ai besoin. (I need one.)

**Position of en:**
| Tense | Position | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Present | Before verb | J'**en** veux. |
| Passé composé | Before auxiliary | J'**en** ai acheté. |
| Near future | Before infinitive | Je vais **en** acheter. |
| Negation | ne + **en** + verb + pas | Je n'**en** veux pas. |
| Imperative (affirm.) | After verb + hyphen | Prends-**en** ! (Take some!) |
| Imperative (neg.) | Before verb | N'**en** prends pas ! |

**Important:** Unlike direct object pronouns, 'en' does NOT trigger past participle agreement:
- Des croissants ? J'en ai acheté. ✓ (NOT achetés)

**Common expressions:**
- **J'en ai marre !** = I'm fed up!
- **Je m'en vais.** = I'm leaving.
- **S'en aller** = to leave / to go away

examples.french: Des boulangeries ? Il y en a deux dans mon quartier.
examples.english: Bakeries? There are two in my neighbourhood.

drills.question: Complete: Tu veux des croissants ? — Oui, j'___ veux trois. (Yes, I want three.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces 'des croissants'. The number stays: j'en veux trois.

drills.question: Where does 'en' go in the near future?
drills.explanation: Near future: en goes before the infinitive. Je vais en acheter.

drills.question: Complete: Tu as besoin d'aide ? — Oui, j'___ ai besoin. (Yes, I need some.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces 'de + noun' after 'avoir besoin de': j'en ai besoin.

drills.question: Does 'en' trigger past participle agreement with avoir?
drills.explanation: Unlike le/la/les, 'en' does NOT trigger agreement: J'en ai acheté (not achetés).

drills.question: Complete: Il y a combien de parcs ? — Il y ___ a trois. (There are three.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces the noun in 'il y a + number + noun': il y en a trois.

drills.question: How do you say 'Take some!' (imperative, informal)?
drills.explanation: Affirmative imperative: verb + hyphen + en: Prends-en !

drills.question: Complete: Des voisins sympathiques ? J'___ connais plusieurs. (Nice neighbours? I know several.)
drills.explanation: 'En' replaces 'des voisins'. Quantity word stays: j'en connais plusieurs.

drills.question: What does 'J'en ai marre !' mean?
drills.explanation: 'En avoir marre' is a common expression meaning 'to be fed up'.

standard.question: What does 'le quartier' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le quartier' is the neighbourhood or district.

standard.question: Fill in the blank: Tu veux du pain ? — Oui, j'___ veux. (Yes, I want some.)
standard.explanation: 'En' replaces 'du pain' (partitive). J'en veux = I want some.

standard.question: What is 'la boulangerie'?
standard.explanation: 'La boulangerie' is the bakery. A key feature of every French neighbourhood!

standard.question: Fill in the blank: Il y a des parcs ? — Oui, il y ___ a deux. (Yes, there are two.)
standard.explanation: 'En' replaces the noun. The number stays: il y en a deux.

standard.question: What does 'le voisin' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le voisin' is the neighbour. Feminine: 'la voisine'.

advanced.question: What does 'le coin' mean in 'le café du coin'?
advanced.explanation: 'Du coin' = local. 'Le café du coin' = the local café.

advanced.question: Fill in the blank: Des croissants ? J'___ ai acheté ce matin. (Croissants? I bought some this morning.)
advanced.explanation: 'En' replaces 'des croissants'. Note: no past participle agreement with 'en'.

advanced.question: What is 'la mairie'?
advanced.explanation: 'La mairie' is the town hall. Civil marriages in France take place here.

advanced.question: Fill in the blank: Tu as besoin d'aide ? — Oui, j'___ ai besoin. (Yes, I need some.)
Fill in the blank: Il y a combien de boulangeries ? — Il y ___ a deux. (There are two.)
advanced.explanation: 'En' in 'il y a' expressions: il y en a + number.

standard.scenario: A new classmate asks about your neighbourhood. Describe where you live — mention the shops, the park, and your neighbours. Use the pronoun 'en' when talking about quantities.
keyPhrases: J'habite dans un quartier… (I live in a… neighbourhood)
Il y en a… (There is/are… of them)
Près de chez moi, il y a… (Near my home, there is…)
standard.modelAnswer: J'habite dans un quartier calme. Il y a une boulangerie — j'en achète du pain tous les matins. Des parcs ? Il y en a deux près de chez moi. La poste est à cinq minutes. J'ai des voisins très gentils — j'en ai quatre sur mon étage. Mon quartier est très agréable !
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'en' naturally three times (j'en achète, il y en a deux, j'en ai quatre), describes the neighbourhood with key vocabulary (quartier, boulangerie, parcs, poste, voisins), and gives a complete picture of the area.
standard.tip: When someone asks 'how many?' or you mention a quantity, use 'en' to avoid repeating the noun. Keep the number at the end!

advanced.scenario: You have just moved to a new neighbourhood and are telling a French friend about it. Describe what shops and facilities are nearby, how many of each there are, and what your neighbours are like. Use 'en' for quantities and 'de + noun' replacements. Mention the town hall and local spots.
keyPhrases: Il y en a… dans mon quartier. (There are… in my neighbourhood.)
J'en ai besoin / J'en connais… (I need some / I know some…)
Au coin de la rue / Près de la mairie (On the corner / Near the town hall)
Des voisins ? J'en ai… (Neighbours? I have…)
advanced.modelAnswer: Mon nouveau quartier est super ! Il y a deux boulangeries — j'en ai testé une hier, le pain est excellent. Des parcs, il y en a un très grand derrière la mairie. La poste est au coin de la rue, j'en ai besoin pour envoyer un colis. Les voisins sont sympathiques — j'en connais déjà trois. Le café du coin est très agréable, j'en viens à l'instant. C'est vraiment un bon quartier !
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'en' five times in varied contexts (j'en ai testé, il y en a un, j'en ai besoin, j'en connais, j'en viens), integrates advanced vocabulary (mairie, coin), and creates a vivid picture of the neighbourhood.
